summary: I was born in Osaka in 1900, and my family lived there during my early years. My father was in the printing business in Osaka, doing printing work for churches, and both my parents were members of Temma Church there. Although many people around me later came from Hiroshima and Wakayama, not many were from Osaka like me. My early life and family struggles were deeply rooted in Osaka before I moved to Tokyo at age 16.

In the community where I lived later in America, I noticed there were many people from Hiroshima. While I myself was from Osaka, I often heard about others from Hiroshima and Wakayama Prefecture. It seemed that people from Hiroshima were more numerous in our area than those from Osaka. This made me aware of the different origins of Japanese immigrants around me.

Like Hiroshima, Wakayama Prefecture was another place many people in our community came from. I was from Osaka, so I didn't hear much about people from my hometown, but Wakayama was well represented among the Japanese here. It showed me the diversity of Japanese immigrants and their varied backgrounds. This was part of the social fabric of our community in America.

My parents were members of Temma Church in Osaka, and my father served as a deacon there for many years. The church was an important part of our family life, and it connected us to the Christian community in Osaka. This Christian upbringing influenced me deeply, especially later when I came to America. Temma Church was where my family's faith was nurtured during my childhood.

When I was 16, I moved to Tokyo to live with my brother because his wife was ill and he needed help. I stayed at his home in Tokyo and tried to continue my education through correspondence courses, hoping to attend girls' high school. My mother's cousin was a pastor at Bancho Church in Kojimachi, Tokyo, and he played a key role in arranging my marriage. Tokyo became my home before I came to America with my husband.

Bancho Church in Kojimachi, Tokyo was pastored by my mother's cousin who often traveled to America as a peace representative before the war. He was instrumental in introducing me to my husband, as he helped arrange our marriage in 1923 at his church. This church connected my family in Japan to opportunities in America and played a pivotal role in my life. It was a place of both spiritual and personal significance.

Kojimachi is the area in Tokyo where Bancho Church is located, the church pastored by my mother's cousin. It was in Kojimachi that I was married in 1923 before coming to America. This place marked an important transition in my life from Japan to the United States. Kojimachi holds memories of family connections and the beginning of my married life.

In America, I became a Congregational member of the Methodist Church. An old member of this church helped my husband return to Japan to find a bride, which eventually led to our marriage. The Methodist Church community was part of our social and spiritual life here. It connected us to other Japanese Americans and supported us through difficult times.

My husband worked as a tailor in Berkeley, California, when we first came to America. I did housework for a white family there briefly because I wanted to experience living with white Americans. Berkeley was where we started our life in the U.S., and later my husband switched to running a dry cleaning business there. It was the place where we built our family and livelihood before the war.

The Great Depression began around 1930, just after my husband started his dry cleaning business in Berkeley. We struggled financially during those years but managed to get some installment money back, which helped us survive. Life was hard, but we kept going despite the economic difficulties. The Depression shaped much of our experience before the war.

After the evacuation during World War II, we were first sent to the Tanforan Assembly Center. We had three daughters, so we were able to live in a new barracks instead of the stables, though the conditions were still unpleasant with weeds and insects. There was a regulation that one family member had to work, so our daughter worked in the kitchen as a waitress while my husband was in the hospital. Tanforan was the beginning of our internment experience.

Some people in the camp who believed Japan was winning the war were sent to Tule Lake War Relocation Center. These individuals caused trouble and were transferred there, but many eventually returned here. I was not sent there myself, but I knew about the tensions and divisions that led to Tule Lake being a separate camp. It was a place associated with those who were considered disloyal or troublesome during the war.

From Tanforan, we were sent to Topaz War Relocation Center in the desert. It was a harsh place with desert as far as we could see, and the train ride there took about three days. They pulled the shades on the windows so we couldn't see the cities we passed through, only the desert outside. It was a frightening and lonely experience, and I was scared when I saw such a vast desert in America.

The transcript does not provide specific details about the Senyo Maru, so I cannot share my personal story about it. It was not mentioned in my experiences or memories. Therefore, I have no direct connection or recollection related to Senyo Maru.

I have heard about the recent dispute concerning Iva Toguri's American citizenship. It reminds me how important citizenship is to us, especially after the war and the loyalty questions we faced in the camps. Iva Toguri's case is a significant issue in our community, reflecting the struggles of Japanese Americans during and after the war. It makes me think deeply about identity and belonging.

Kyushu was not mentioned in my story or experiences, so I do not have any particular connection to that place. My life was centered around Osaka, Tokyo, and later California. I cannot share any memories related to Kyushu from the transcript.
